topic.title: Expectation-Maximization
topic.question: How does the EM algorithm find local maxima of likelihood for latent-variable models, and why does it always converge?

promptVariant.name: Whiteboard prompt v4 - board state coherence check

lesson.title: EM: Climbing Likelihood Without Seeing the Labels
lesson.learnerLevel: graduate students and ML practitioners familiar with MLE and basic probability
lesson.payoff: EM fits a K-component mixture model in O(N*K) work per iteration; the naive brute-force enumeration over all hidden-label assignments would require 2^N evaluations -- exponential in the dataset size.
staircase: latent-variable models and why direct MLE is intractable
the log-likelihood decomposed into ELBO plus KL divergence
E-step: choosing q to collapse the KL gap to zero
M-step: maximizing the ELBO over theta to raise the likelihood
the monotone convergence guarantee: each full iteration never decreases the log-likelihood
concrete GMM responsibility formula instantiating the abstract steps
lesson.destination: You will hold the identity log p(X;theta) = ELBO(q,theta) + KL(q || p(Z|X,theta)), understand that the E-step sets q = p(Z|X,theta^old) making KL=0 so ELBO equals the log-likelihood, and that the M-step then maximizes that ELBO over theta, proving ell(theta^new) >= ell(theta^old) at every iteration.

narration.fullText: Suppose your data X were produced by a latent-variable model -- a data-generating process, meaning the probability model assumed to have produced the observations, that involves hidden variables Z you never observe. In a Gaussian mixture model, Z is the unobserved cluster assignment for each point. Direct MLE -- maximum-likelihood estimation, choosing the parameter value theta that makes the observed data most probable -- requires summing over all possible Z, giving a log of a sum that resists closed-form optimization. A brute-force search over all 2^N label assignments is exponential and completely infeasible. EM escapes this trap through a geometric insight. The log-likelihood ell of theta decomposes exactly as ell(theta) equals ELBO(q, theta) plus KL(q parallel p(Z given X, theta)). Here ELBO stands for evidence lower bound -- a function of both a distribution q over Z and the parameter theta that sits below the log-likelihood and is computable without evaluating that intractable sum. KL divergence measures how much the auxiliary distribution q differs from the true posterior over Z; it is always non-negative, so the ELBO is always a lower bound on ell. Picture this on the board: a smooth log-likelihood curve over a theta axis, and below it a lower-bound curve that touches the likelihood at one point. The gap between the curves at any theta is exactly the KL divergence. The E-step chooses q to be the exact posterior p(Z given X, theta^old), which drives KL to zero, making the ELBO kiss the likelihood curve at theta^old. The M-step then finds theta^new as the argmax -- the argument, meaning the input value, that produces the largest output -- of the ELBO over theta. Because the ELBO is now equal to the likelihood at theta^old and we are moving theta to increase it, we are guaranteed ell(theta^new) is at least ell(theta^old). This is the convergence proof in one sentence: each M-step maximizes a function that equals the log-likelihood at the current point, so the sequence of likelihoods is monotone non-decreasing. In the GMM concretely, with K components and mixing weights pi_k, the E-step computes responsibilities r_ik equals pi_k times N(x_i; mu_k, Sigma_k) divided by the sum over j of pi_j times N(x_i; mu_j, Sigma_j) -- that fraction is the posterior probability that point i came from cluster k. The M-step re-estimates mu_k as the responsibility-weighted mean of all points. The algorithm iterates until ell stops changing: no exponential search, just O(N times K) arithmetic per iteration, converging to a local maximum.

beats.text: Suppose your data X were produced by a latent-variable model -- a data-generating process, meaning the probability model assumed to have produced the observations, that involves hidden variables Z you never observe. In a Gaussian mixture model, Z is the unobserved cluster assignment for each point.
beats.whyThisBeat: latent-variable models and why direct MLE is intractable

beats.text: Direct MLE -- maximum-likelihood estimation, choosing the parameter value theta that makes the observed data most probable -- requires summing over all possible Z, giving a log of a sum with no closed form. Brute-force enumeration over all 2^N label assignments is exponential and completely infeasible.
beats.whyThisBeat: latent-variable models and why direct MLE is intractable

beats.text: EM escapes this trap through a geometric insight. The log-likelihood ell of theta decomposes exactly as ell(theta) equals ELBO(q, theta) plus KL(q parallel p(Z given X, theta)).
beats.whyThisBeat: the log-likelihood decomposed into ELBO plus KL divergence

beats.text: Here ELBO stands for evidence lower bound -- a function of both an auxiliary distribution q over Z and the parameter theta that sits below the log-likelihood and is computable without evaluating the intractable sum. KL divergence measures how much q differs from the true posterior; it is always non-negative, so the ELBO is always a lower bound.
beats.whyThisBeat: the log-likelihood decomposed into ELBO plus KL divergence

beats.text: Picture this: a smooth log-likelihood curve over a theta axis, and below it a lower-bound curve. The gap between them at any theta is the KL divergence. They touch at exactly one point -- the current theta.
beats.whyThisBeat: the log-likelihood decomposed into ELBO plus KL divergence

beats.text: The E-step chooses q to be the exact posterior p(Z given X, theta^old), which drives KL to zero, making the ELBO kiss the likelihood curve at theta^old. Now the lower bound and the likelihood are equal at the current parameter.
beats.whyThisBeat: E-step: choosing q to collapse the KL gap to zero

beats.text: The M-step then finds theta^new as the argmax -- the input value that produces the largest output -- of the ELBO over theta. Because the ELBO equals the log-likelihood at theta^old and we move theta to increase it, ell(theta^new) is at least ell(theta^old).
beats.whyThisBeat: M-step: maximizing the ELBO over theta to raise the likelihood

beats.text: This is the convergence proof: each M-step maximizes a function that equals the log-likelihood at the current point, so the sequence of likelihoods is monotone non-decreasing and converges to a local maximum.
beats.whyThisBeat: the monotone convergence guarantee: each full iteration never decreases the log-likelihood

beats.text: In a Gaussian mixture with K components and mixing weights pi_k, the E-step computes the responsibility r_ik -- the posterior probability that point i came from cluster k -- as pi_k times N(x_i; mu_k, Sigma_k) divided by the normalizing sum over all components.
beats.whyThisBeat: concrete GMM responsibility formula instantiating the abstract steps

beats.text: The M-step re-estimates mu_k as the responsibility-weighted mean of all points. The algorithm iterates until ell stops changing: no exponential search, just O(N times K) arithmetic per iteration, converging to a local maximum.
beats.whyThisBeat: concrete GMM responsibility formula instantiating the abstract steps

beats.text: So EM is a lift-and-shift: E lifts the bound to touch the likelihood, M shifts theta to the new peak of that bound. Monotone convergence is baked into the geometry.
beats.whyThisBeat: the monotone convergence guarantee: each full iteration never decreases the log-likelihood

actions.content: Latent-variable model: observe X, hidden Z

Smooth concave log-likelihood curve ell(theta) plotted over a horizontal theta axis spanning the full width; below it a second smooth curve labeled ELBO(q,theta) with a smaller hump; a vertical double-headed arrow between the two curves at a theta value three-quarters from the left, labeled KL gap; the two curves meet and touch at a point labeled theta^old with a filled dot
underline ELBO(q,theta) in blue to flag it as the computable lower bound
circle KL(q || p(Z|X,theta)) in red to flag it as the always-nonnegative gap
draw a red double-headed arrow between the ell curve and the ELBO curve at the KL-gap position, labeling it KL >= 0

A copy of the touching-point region of the likelihood_bound_diagram, zoomed in: the ell curve and ELBO curve now coincide at theta^old (gap collapses to zero), with a bracket labeled E-step: q = p(Z|X,theta^old), KL=0 pointing to the contact point

On the shared theta axis of likelihood_bound_diagram, a rightward curved arrow from theta^old to a new point theta^new further right where the ELBO curve peaks, labeled M-step: argmax_theta ELBO; the ell curve is visibly higher at theta^new than at theta^old, with a small upward bracket labeled ell increases
box the entire inequality ell(theta^new) >= ell(theta^old) in green as the convergence guarantee
r_ik = [pi_k * N(x_i; mu_k, Sigma_k)] / [sum_j pi_j * N(x_i; mu_j, Sigma_j)]
underline the numerator pi_k * N(x_i; mu_k, Sigma_k) in blue as the unnormalized posterior contribution of component k
EM: E lifts bound to touch ell -- M shifts theta to new peak -- monotone convergence
box the phrase monotone convergence in green

codeBlocks.code:
import numpy as np
from scipy.stats import multivariate_normal

def em_gmm(X, K, n_iter=50):
    N, D = X.shape
    pi = np.ones(K) / K
    mu = X[np.random.choice(N, K, replace=False)]
    Sigma = [np.eye(D) for _ in range(K)]

    for _ in range(n_iter):
        log_r = np.array([
            np.log(pi[k] + 1e-300) + multivariate_normal.logpdf(X, mu[k], Sigma[k])
            for k in range(K)
        ]).T
        log_r -= log_r.max(axis=1, keepdims=True)
        r = np.exp(log_r); r /= r.sum(axis=1, keepdims=True)
        Nk = r.sum(axis=0)
        pi = Nk / N
        mu = (r.T @ X) / Nk[:, None]
        for k in range(K):
            diff = X - mu[k]
            Sigma[k] = (r[:, k:k+1] * diff).T @ diff / Nk[k]

    return pi, mu, Sigma, r
codeBlocks.purpose: Makes the abstract E-step responsibility formula and M-step weighted-mean update concrete and runnable for a K-component Gaussian mixture, showing how O(N*K) arithmetic per iteration replaces exponential enumeration
